result:
The morning I learned that the name for the small, moon-shaped scar between my thumb and index finger is called a “lunula burn” was the morning everything felt briefly astonishing, as though language had crept into the bathroom mirror to braid itself around the ordinary. I’d never met the dermatologist before—Dr. Reese, abrupt and soft-voiced at once—who identified the pale crescent as definitive evidence of a childhood encounter with a hot cookie sheet. She said, almost apologetically, “It’s common in right-handed toddlers who insist on pulling their own tray of dinosaur nuggets from the oven.” The room suddenly tilted; I could see my three-year-old self gripping an oven door like the mouth of a dragon, bathed in tiny triumph and peril larger than my vocabulary. Afterwards I stood at the pharmacy counter waiting for antibiotic ointment, watching middle-aged men in suits furtively buy chewing gum and antacids, all of us pretending we weren’t carrying younger selves around in the hollows of our wrists, in the tender folds of receipts and pill bottles.

I think of memory as coastlines. Every recollection is a weathered cliff; erosion keeps making new shapes, but the original rock is still there beneath the lichen. After the dermatologist appointment I drove home past a redwood forest, fogsack hanging low, and the smell of damp tannin surged all at once into childhood campouts. I was seven again, flicking pine needles at my brother Lo who swore needles could become arrows if you believed hard enough. We built a lean-to with a blue tarp and told each other that when the mist veiled everything, the forest became ghost mail—messages you could only read by hushing your own pulse. Lo died at twenty-two in a river he already knew like his own bloodstream, but every redwood exhale is still an unopened letter.

At a rest stop I bought coffee bitter enough to remind me why I usually don’t. An older woman in a fleece patterned with constellations asked to share my bench. She opened a Tupperware of persimmon cookies and began talking as if continuing a conversation we’d started in a past life. She said her husband’s old pilot watch ticked too loudly now that he was gone; sometimes she wore it in the breast pocket of her flannel so every brief, insistent beat filled the hollow where he’d once rested his chin. I offered no equivalent grief inventory. Instead I told her about lunula burns and redwood ghosts. She listened then broke a cookie in half—one pale shard looked exactly like a thumbnail moon—and handed it to me. “Time and scar tissue,” she said. “Same material, really.”

When I reached my apartment, dusk was smearing violet along the blinds. My neighbor the saxophonist was practicing scales that rose and wilted like deep-sea algae. I stood on the landing, keys biting my palm, suddenly pierced by how any hour accumulates legions of exiles: childhood toys abandoned under floorboards, the particular smell of my mother’s perm solution on rainy afternoons, the vapor from hospital corridors where Lo gave me two thumbs-up before vanishing into anesthetic twilight. All of it settles inside us, compound interest accruing until we wake one routine morning and discover paler moons tattooed on our anatomy like stamps from private tributaries.

Inside, I turned on one lamp and sat without taking off my coat. On the wall: a calendar whose squares resembled empty rabbit cages. November’s photograph showed bioluminescent plankton swirling a Maldivian tide; the longer I stared, the more the blue looked like bruise blooming under skin. I remembered reading that bioluminescence is a defense mechanism—the tiny organisms light up when threatened, equivalent to screaming in the only language available. What ecstatic surrender: to glow when being eaten. Or perhaps it’s a love letter aimed at the universe regardless of witness. Either way, it’s visible for miles across black water.

My phone had eleven notifications demanding nothing important. I silenced it and pulled a box from the closet: letters. They smelled faintly of attic dust and cedar. The top one had my grandmother’s handwriting—cornstalk scrawl steep as her turquoise cane. She used to say that sentences are lassos flung into the dark; what you drag back reveals more about the valley of the thrower than the size of the beast. In the letter she apologized for misplacing her hearing aid, which meant she’d spent Easter eavesdropping mostly on memory. She closed with a recipe for lemon cake written so small you had to squint like an astronomer: fold the batter with the same motion you’d use if whisking away a miracle that insists on staying.

Next was a postcard Lo mailed from Banff—picture of a glacial lake bluer than sincerity. On the reverse he’d signed only Love, L. as though his whole continuum could funnel into that single letter, calligraphy lean and loop-winged like osprey script. How cruel that stamps are the geometry of distance. Beneath the postcard I unearthed the ticket stub from the last concert we attended together: Radiohead playing “Fake Plastic Trees” while Lo cried into his sleeve, not because of the song but because for three minutes the stadium was a single lung inhaling in unison. He kept the stub between pages of All the King’s Men; when I closed it stub fluttered like a moth refusing to die.

After an hour the saxophonist tapered into silence, replaced by distant sirens—someone else’s emergency. I opened my notebook intending to write about lunula burns, but instead found myself transcribing a dream I hadn’t known I’d had: a nursery where newborns are assigned equations. Nurses carry calculators like bottles. One crib holds me—except in dream-country I am translucent and growing more so. The nurse scrolls across my curvature, frowns. “Depreciating value,” she mutters. “Needs more story.” She presses an ink thumbprint directly onto the moon scar. Whereupon the blueprint of my bones floods with color. I become dense, glorious, mortal singing inside marrow.

Dream transcription exhausted itself; ink puddled like squid defense. I reread the paragraph twice, startled by how little fiction clung to it. We keep making offerings at altars of catalogued memory, but some doors only open via messier courier. Hence the woman with ticking flannel and persimmon fragments. Hence glowing panic and letter-lassos and fever blue calendars.

At 2 a.m. I microwaved leftover curry, turmeric staining the bowl the hue of old bruised light. The apartment heater hiccupped like an amateur clock. I set Lo’s postcard propped against the salt shaker: Love, L. An incision masquerading as signature. I spoke aloud to the empty kitchen, “Okay, we’re still here.” Outside, fog pressed city windows inward like the slow palms of a careful librarian shutting the world’s rarest book.

I thought then about the lunula burn—how the body keeps rewriting itself with both disaster and repair—and decided to name tonight’s fog Dr. Reese, in honor of strangers who translate our scars into narrative roofing. We trespass through ourselves, leaving breadcrumb scars, breadcrumb blue plankton, breadcrumb clock ticks, until one day some future child sponges at a kitchen table and finds temperature imprinted on their own skin, wondering why moons keep attaching themselves to flesh. Maybe they’ll run water over the mark, maybe they’ll flinch or maybe they’ll marvel, but either way they’ll pause mid-bite of cereal and experience the aperture yawning open between what happened and what is told.

One thousand words, the screen ticks, as if counting heartbeats. I could keep excavating: the way wind flutes around parking structures like orchestras rehearsing apocalypse; how my mother’s perfume still haunts the crook of my elbow some Thursdays; Lo’s irises the exact green of oxidized copper. But the café downstairs is about to switch off its sign; the neon cicada dims to a sleepy ember. Enough. The herbaceous scent of ointment on the lunula scar smells like both wound and orchard. I rub it in, feeling galaxies rearrange their paperwork. Tomorrow I will mail the dermatologist a thank-you note and include the half moon cookie traced on cardstock. Because after all, time and scar tissue are the same material once you speak kindly to them.

And because somewhere a child is already reaching for an oven handle—our geometries perpetually passing the whisper forward—language trails behind like phosphorescence, glowing not to survive but simply to say: Look.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
